title: Synthesising treatment effects across DiD, RCT, and pre-post designs
text: simulate 30 studies known hierarchical model: true population treatment effect -0.15 raw scale. baseline normalisation (default), corresponds approximately -0.333 normalised scale.
code: library(metadid) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)  sim <- simulate_meta_did(   n_studies     = 30,   n_control     = 80,   n_treatment   = 80,   true_effect   = -0.15,   sigma_effect  = 0.03,   true_trend    = -0.02,   sigma_trend   = 0.01,   baseline_mean = 0.45,   baseline_sd   = 0.02,   rho           = 0.5,   seed          = 4718 )

title: Synthesising treatment effects across DiD, RCT, and pre-post designs
text: fitting models, useful see data-generating process directly. plot shows study pair faint lines (control treatment group means pre post), grand mean overlaid bold. treatment group starts roughly level control group drops much — gap two bold slopes treatment effect. spread faint lines reflects -study heterogeneity.
code: study_means <- sim |>   group_by(study_id, group, time) |>   summarise(mean_value = mean(value), .groups = "drop") |>   mutate(time = factor(time, levels = c("pre", "post")))  grand_means <- study_means |>   group_by(group, time) |>   summarise(mean_value = mean(mean_value), .groups = "drop")  ggplot(study_means, aes(x = time, y = mean_value, colour = group,                         group = interaction(study_id, group))) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.15) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.15, size = 1) +   geom_line(data = grand_means, aes(group = group), linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(data = grand_means, aes(group = group), size = 3) +   labs(x = "Time", y = "Mean outcome", colour = "Group") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Synthesising treatment effects across DiD, RCT, and pre-post designs
text: compare two ways using underlying data.

what: Scenario A: all studies treated as DiD
title: Synthesising treatment effects across DiD, RCT, and pre-post designs
text: best case, every study provides full four-cell summary statistics (pre/post × control/treatment). gives model maximum information per study.
code: all_did <- as_summary_did(sim)  fit_all_did <- meta_did(   summary_data  = all_did,   seed          = 4718 )  print(fit_all_did) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 30 | RCT = 0 | Pre-Post = 0 | DiD (change only) = 0  #> Population treatment effect: -0.351  90% CI [-0.371, -0.332]

title: Synthesising treatment effects across DiD, RCT, and pre-post designs
text: Now suppose , underlying data, third studies provide full information. Another third RCTs (post-treatment outcomes ), remaining third pre-post studies (treatment arm , control group).
code: study_ids <- unique(sim$study_id) did_ids <- study_ids[1:10] rct_ids <- study_ids[11:20] pp_ids  <- study_ids[21:30]  # Preserve true_params for subsetting true_params <- attr(sim, "true_params")  sim_did <- sim |> filter(study_id %in% did_ids) sim_rct <- sim |> filter(study_id %in% rct_ids) sim_pp  <- sim |> filter(study_id %in% pp_ids)  attr(sim_did, "true_params") <- true_params |> filter(study_id %in% did_ids) attr(sim_rct, "true_params") <- true_params |> filter(study_id %in% rct_ids) attr(sim_pp, "true_params")  <- true_params |> filter(study_id %in% pp_ids)  mixed <- bind_rows(   as_summary_did(sim_did),   as_summary_rct(sim_rct),   as_summary_pp(sim_pp) )  fit_mixed <- meta_did(   summary_data = mixed,   seed         = 4718 )  print(fit_mixed) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 10 | RCT = 10 | Pre-Post = 10 | DiD (change only) = 0  #> Population treatment effect: -0.350  90% CI [-0.372, -0.328]

title: Synthesising treatment effects across DiD, RCT, and pre-post designs
text: fits recover true normalised effect (-0.333, dashed line). mixed-design posterior slightly wider, reflecting information lost two-thirds studies provide incomplete data — difference modest.  key takeaway: assuming common latent structure, model borrows strength across designs. RCT pre-post studies contribute meaningful information treatment effect, even though observe less underlying process full study.
code: draws_did <- as.numeric(   fit_all_did$fit$draws("treatment_effect_mean", format = "draws_matrix") ) draws_mix <- as.numeric(   fit_mixed$fit$draws("treatment_effect_mean", format = "draws_matrix") )  comp_df <- data.frame(   value = c(draws_did, draws_mix),   scenario = rep(c("All DiD", "Mixed designs"), each = length(draws_did)) )  ggplot(comp_df, aes(x = value, fill = scenario)) +   geom_density(alpha = 0.4) +   geom_vline(xintercept = -0.15 / 0.45, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +   annotate("text", x = -0.15 / 0.45 + 0.003, y = Inf, label = "True effect",            hjust = 0, vjust = 1.5, size = 3.5) +   labs(x = "Population treatment effect", y = "Density", fill = NULL) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Synthesising treatment effects across DiD, RCT, and pre-post designs
text: calibration check, pp_check_cdf(type = "summary") compares empirical CDF observed study-level treatment effects (step function) posterior predictive CDF (ribbon dashed median). model well-specified, observed ECDF fall within predictive band.
code: pp_check_cdf(fit_mixed, type = "summary")

title: Synthesising treatment effects across DiD, RCT, and pre-post designs
text: granular view, pp_check_effects() shows study’s observed naive treatment effect (vertical line) posterior predictive density study. useful identifying individual studies model struggles explain.
code: pp_check_effects(fit_mixed)

title: Model details and identification
text: metadid assumes every study — regardless design — arises common latent difference--differences () data-generating process. study ii, outcomes control group follow (Yi,c,preYi,c,post)∼𝒩[(αiαi+βi),(σi,c,pre2ρi,cσi,c,preσi,c,postρi,cσi,c,preσi,c,postσi,c,post2)], \begin{pmatrix} Y_{,c,\mathrm{pre}} \\ Y_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i \\ \alpha_i + \beta_i \end{pmatrix} , \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{,c,\mathrm{pre}} & \rho_{,c}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \\ \rho_{,c}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{post}} & \sigma^2_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \right], outcomes treatment group follow (Yi,t,preYi,t,post)∼𝒩[(αi+γiαi+γi+βi+θi),(σi,t,pre2ρi,tσi,t,preσi,t,postρi,tσi,t,preσi,t,postσi,t,post2)]. \begin{pmatrix} Y_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} \\ Y_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i + \gamma_i \\ \alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i \end{pmatrix} , \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} & \rho_{,t}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \\ \rho_{,t}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{post}} & \sigma^2_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \right]. parameters : αi\alpha_i: baseline mean control group βi\beta_i: time trend shared across groups γi\gamma_i: baseline difference treatment control groups θi\theta_i: study-specific treatment effect (estimand) ρi,c\rho_{,c}, ρi,t\rho_{,t}: pre/post correlations within group σi,g,t\sigma_{,g,t}: marginal standard deviations key identifying assumption , absent treatment, treatment group followed time trend βi\beta_i control group (.e., parallel trends assumption).

title: Model details and identification
text: Different study designs observe different subsets latent bivariate structure: design sees fewer cells, less ability separate parameters α\alpha, β\beta, γ\gamma, θ\theta.

title: Model details and identification
text: studies observe four cells 2×2 table. mean structure , four expected cell means : Taking double difference expected values gives (μi,t,post−μi,t,pre)−(μi,c,post−μi,c,pre)=(βi+θi)−βi=θi. (\mu_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}) - (\mu_{,c,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}) = (\beta_i + \theta_i) - \beta_i = \theta_i. time trend βi\beta_i cancels, baseline difference γi\gamma_i cancels, leaving θi\theta_i cleanly identified. studies anchor entire model.

what: DiD (change only) studies: identified but without levels
title: Model details and identification
text: studies report mean change time (post minus pre) arm, without reporting level means separately. expected change scores βi\beta_i (control) βi+θi\beta_i + \theta_i (treatment), difference change scores still identifies θi\theta_i. However, pre-treatment levels observed, studies anchor normalisation baseline (see ).

what: RCT studies: treatment effect confounded with baseline differences
title: Model details and identification
text: RCT studies observe post-treatment outcomes arms. expected difference arms μi,t,post−μi,c,post=(αi+γi+βi+θi)−(αi+βi)=γi+θi. \mu_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,c,\mathrm{post}} = (\alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i) - (\alpha_i + \beta_i) = \gamma_i + \theta_i. Without pre-treatment data, θi\theta_i confounded baseline difference γi\gamma_i. Randomisation makes γi\gamma_i small expectation, model separate two data single RCT alone.

what: Pre-post studies: treatment effect confounded with time trends
title: Model details and identification
text: Pre-post studies observe treatment arm time points. expected change time μi,t,post−μi,t,pre=(αi+γi+βi+θi)−(αi+γi)=βi+θi. \mu_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} = (\alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i) - (\alpha_i + \gamma_i) = \beta_i + \theta_i. Without control arm, θi\theta_i confounded time trend βi\beta_i.

title: Model details and identification
text: Without studies, treatment effect identified data. meta_did() stop error studies present. check can overridden allow_no_did = TRUE, resulting posterior driven primarily priors rather data. studies present alongside RCT pre-post studies, hierarchical model propagates information: time trend distribution estimated studies informs pre-post decomposition, baseline structure studies informs RCT decomposition. cross-design borrowing core value metadid approach.

title: Model details and identification
text: default (normalise_by_baseline = TRUE), meta_did() normalises outcome data design-appropriate baseline mean fitting. places studies common relative scale, making treatment effect interpretable proportional change.

title: Model details and identification
text: pre-post studies, normalisation divides pre-treatment quantity, relevant baseline parameter known exactly 1 fixed rather estimated. time trend treatment effect remain free parameters. change-studies report change scores (post minus pre) rather level means, lack study-specific pre-treatment level normalise . Instead, normalisation divides change scores grand mean pre-control means across full studies meta-analysis. places change scores relative scale designs, requires least full studies present. RCT studies, pre-treatment data, normalisation divides post-treatment control mean. fixes sum αi+βi=1\alpha_i + \beta_i = 1, fix αi\alpha_i βi\beta_i individually. Since sum determined, model fixes αi=1\alpha_i = 1 βi=0\beta_i = 0 — simplest split consistent constraint. treatment effect simplifies : θi=y‾,t,post−1 \theta_i = \bar{y}_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - 1 modelling choice, consequence data. means normalised RCT treatment effects interpreted relative control post-treatment mean, time-trend adjustment. appropriate primary goal cross-design synthesis anchored studies, provide time-trend information.

title: Model details and identification
text: normalisation, population treatment effect μθ\mu_\theta expressed units relative baseline. example, μθ=−0.33\mu_\theta = -0.33 means 33% reduction relative baseline level.

title: Model details and identification
text: Study-specific treatment effects drawn population distribution: θi∼𝒩(μθ,τθ2), \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\theta, \tau_\theta^2), μθ\mu_\theta overall treatment effect (primary quantity interest) τθ\tau_\theta captures -study heterogeneity. study-level parameters (time trends βi\beta_i, baseline differences γi\gamma_i) similarly share population-level priors. Pre-post correlations ρ\rho can modelled hierarchically via Fisher-zz transform hierarchical_rho = TRUE.

title: Model details and identification
text: design_effects = TRUE, model allows population treatment effect mean differ systematically design: μθ,RCT=μθ+δRCT,μθ,PP=μθ+δPP \mu_{\theta,\text{RCT}} = \mu_\theta + \delta_{\text{RCT}}, \quad \mu_{\theta,\text{PP}} = \mu_\theta + \delta_{\text{PP}} δRCT\delta_{\text{RCT}} δPP\delta_{\text{PP}} estimated offsets. relaxes assumption designs estimate exactly estimand, may appropriate selection effects time trends differ systematically across designs.

title: Model details and identification
text: robust_heterogeneity = TRUE, treatment effect distribution uses Student-tt instead normal: θi∼tν(μθ,τθ2) \theta_i \sim t_\nu(\mu_\theta, \tau_\theta^2) ν\nu (degrees freedom) estimated. accommodates outlier studies otherwise inflate τθ\tau_\theta.

title: Model details and identification
text: Priors can customised via set_priors(). See ?set_priors full list parameters defaults.

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: metadid R package Bayesian meta-analysis synthesises treatment effects across studies different designs: difference--differences (), randomised controlled trials (RCT), pre-post studies. uses hierarchical Stan model accounts design-specific information heterogeneity across studies, optional baseline normalisation place outcomes common fractional scale.

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: metadid assumes studies arise common latent difference--differences () structure. Different study designs correspond observing different parts latent structure. studies design framework directly identify treatment effect. Meta-analyses include studies identified data depend entirely modelling assumptions. recommend using approach absence evidence.

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: study ii, outcomes control group satisfy (Yi,c,preYi,c,post)∼𝒩[(αiαi+βi),(σi,c,pre2ρi,cσi,c,preσi,c,postρi,cσi,c,preσi,c,postσi,c,post2)], \begin{pmatrix} Y_{,c,\mathrm{pre}} \\ Y_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i \\ \alpha_i + \beta_i \end{pmatrix} , \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{,c,\mathrm{pre}} & \rho_{,c}\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \\ \rho_{,c}\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{post}} & \sigma^2_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \right], outcomes treatment group satisfy (Yi,t,preYi,t,post)∼𝒩[(αi+γiαi+γi+βi+θi),(σi,t,pre2ρi,tσi,t,preσi,t,postρi,tσi,t,preσi,t,postσi,t,post2)]. \begin{pmatrix} Y_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} \\ Y_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i + \gamma_i \\ \alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i \end{pmatrix} , \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} & \rho_{,t}\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \\ \rho_{,t}\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{post}} & \sigma^2_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \right]. : αi\alpha_i: baseline mean control group βi\beta_i: time trend shared across groups γi\gamma_i: baseline difference treatment control θi\theta_i: study-specific treatment effect ρi,c\rho_{,c}, ρi,t\rho_{,t}: pre/post correlations σi,g,pre\sigma_{,g,\mathrm{pre}}, σi,g,post\sigma_{,g,\mathrm{post}}: marginal standard deviations key identifying assumption , absence treatment, treatment group followed time trend βi\beta_i control group.

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: Different study designs correspond observing subsets latent structure: / RCT: groups time points observed Pre-post: treatment group Post-/ change-score: partial observations levels differences Inference incomplete designs relies shared latent structure across studies.

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: Study-specific treatment effects modelled hierarchically, example θi∼𝒩(μθ,τθ2), \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\theta, \tau_\theta^2), μθ\mu_\theta overall treatment effect τθ\tau_\theta captures -study heterogeneity. robustness outlying study effects, model can alternatively use Student-t distribution, θi∼tν(μθ,τθ), \theta_i \sim t_\nu(\mu_\theta, \tau_\theta), ν\nu controls tail-heaviness.

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: model supports : individual-level data, summary statistics (means, variances, sample sizes) deriving likelihoods bivariate normal model.

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: Designs missing components (e.g. pre-post) become informative borrowing structure studies, increases reliance modelling assumptions .

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: metadid depends cmdstanr instantiate, compile Stan models package install time. Install first haven’t already: install metadid GitHub:
code: install.packages("cmdstanr", repos = c("https://mc-stan.org/r-packages/", getOption("repos"))) cmdstanr::install_cmdstan()  install.packages("instantiate") # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ben18785/metadid")

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: simulate_meta_did() generates individual-level pre/post data arms across set studies known hierarchical model. as_summary_did() aggregates four-cell summary statistics (pre/post × control/treatment) represent typically reported published study. true population treatment effect -0.15 raw scale, approximately -0.333 normalising baseline mean 0.45.
code: library(metadid) #>  #> Attaching package: 'metadid' #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     gamma  sim <- simulate_meta_did(   n_studies     = 20,   true_effect   = -0.15,   sigma_effect  = 0.03,   baseline_mean = 0.45,   rho           = 0.5,   seed          = 42 )  studies <- as_summary_did(sim) head(studies) #> # A tibble: 6 × 13 #>   study_id design n_control n_treatment mean_pre_control mean_post_control #>   <chr>    <chr>      <int>       <int>            <dbl>             <dbl> #> 1 study_1  did          100         100            0.450             0.418 #> 2 study_10 did          100         100            0.448             0.435 #> 3 study_11 did          100         100            0.420             0.409 #> 4 study_12 did          100         100            0.475             0.444 #> 5 study_13 did          100         100            0.447             0.435 #> 6 study_14 did          100         100            0.461             0.416 #> # ℹ 7 more variables: sd_pre_control <dbl>, sd_post_control <dbl>, #> #   mean_pre_treatment <dbl>, mean_post_treatment <dbl>, #> #   sd_pre_treatment <dbl>, sd_post_treatment <dbl>, rho <dbl>

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: meta_did() fits hierarchical Bayesian model. Setting method = "optimize" finds maximum posteriori (MAP) estimate via L-BFGS — much faster full MCMC useful quick iteration.
code: fit <- meta_did(   summary_data = studies,   method       = "optimize",   seed         = 42 )  print(fit) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 20 | RCT = 0 | Pre-Post = 0 | DiD (change only) = 0  #> Population treatment effect: -0.303  (MAP estimate, no uncertainty)

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: summary() returns data frame population- study-level parameters. MAP optimisation, point estimates available (sd, lo, hi NA); use method = "sample" full posterior uncertainty.
code: summary(fit) #>                       parameter       mean sd lo hi #> 1         treatment_effect_mean -0.3032553 NA NA NA #> 2           treatment_effect_sd  0.2621790 NA NA NA #> 3  treatment_effect_did_summary -0.2626185 NA NA NA #> 4  treatment_effect_did_summary -0.4008142 NA NA NA #> 5  treatment_effect_did_summary -0.1788253 NA NA NA #> ...

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: full posterior uncertainty, use default method = "sample": summary() now includes posterior standard deviations credible intervals:
code: fit_mcmc <- meta_did(   summary_data  = studies,   seed          = 42,   iter_warmup   = 200,   iter_sampling = 400 )  print(fit_mcmc) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 20 | RCT = 0 | Pre-Post = 0 | DiD (change only) = 0  #> Population treatment effect: -0.307  90% CI [-0.341, -0.273] summary(fit_mcmc) #>                       parameter        mean         sd          lo         hi #> 1         treatment_effect_mean -0.30734301 0.02053925 -0.34109011 -0.2731060 #> 2           treatment_effect_sd  0.08387904 0.01614552  0.06170731  0.1124974 #> 3  treatment_effect_did_summary -0.23482038 0.02605055 -0.27537815 -0.1916570 #> 4  treatment_effect_did_summary -0.35087501 0.02539211 -0.39315193 -0.3085825 #> 5  treatment_effect_did_summary -0.22512112 0.02910410 -0.27397179 -0.1791810 #> ...

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: pp_check_cdf(type = "summary") compares empirical CDF observed study-level treatment effects (step function) posterior predictive CDF (ribbon dashed median). model well-calibrated, observed ECDF track predictive band.  granular per-study view, pp_check_effects() shows study’s observed naive effect posterior predictive density:
code: pp_check_cdf(fit_mcmc, type = "summary") pp_check_effects(fit_mcmc)

what: Fit a Bayesian meta-analysis model across study designs — meta_did
title: Fit a Bayesian meta-analysis model across study designs — meta_did
text: Fits hierarchical Bayesian model synthesising treatment effects studies different designs: difference--differences (), randomised controlled trials (RCT), pre-post studies. designs contribute shared population treatment effect.

title: Fit a Bayesian meta-analysis model across study designs — meta_did
code: meta_did(   summary_data = NULL,   individual_data = NULL,   normalise_by_baseline = TRUE,   robust_heterogeneity = FALSE,   design_effects = FALSE,   hierarchical_rho = TRUE,   priors = set_priors(),   method = c("sample", "optimize"),   chains = 4L,   iter_warmup = 1000L,   iter_sampling = 1000L,   seed = NULL,   allow_no_did = FALSE,   ... )

title: Fit a Bayesian meta-analysis model across study designs — meta_did
text: summary_data data frame one row per study containing summary statistics. Must include columns study_id design. See validate_summary_data() full column specification per design. Valid designs: "", "did_change", "rct", "pp". individual_data data frame long format one row per observation. Must include columns study_id, design, group, time, value. Valid designs: "", "rct", "pp". study_id may appear summary_data individual_data. normalise_by_baseline Logical. TRUE (default), means SDs divided study's pre-treatment control mean (grand mean change-studies), placing outcomes common fractional scale. robust_heterogeneity Logical. TRUE, study-level treatment effects drawn Student-t distribution rather normal, providing robustness outlier studies. degrees--freedom parameter estimated prior specified priors$nu. design_effects Logical. TRUE, additive offsets population treatment effect mean estimated RCT Pre-Post studies relative (reference). Useful testing whether designs yield systematically different effect estimates. hierarchical_rho Logical. TRUE (default), pre-post correlation modelled hierarchically across studies. Studies reported correlation inform population distribution; studies without one correlation imputed. priors did_priors object set_priors(). Controls prior distributions population-level parameters. method Inference method. "sample" (default) runs full MCMC via Stan's HMC-NUTS sampler returns posterior distribution. "optimize" finds maximum posteriori (MAP) estimate via L-BFGS substantially faster, returns point estimate uncertainty quantification. chains Number MCMC chains. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 4. iter_warmup Number warmup iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. iter_sampling Number sampling iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. seed Integer random seed reproducibility. Default NULL. allow_no_did Logical. FALSE (default), meta_did() stop error studies present, treatment effect identified data without double-difference structure. Set TRUE override check understand limitation (posterior prior-driven). ... Additional arguments passed underlying CmdStanModel method: $sample() method = "sample" (e.g., parallel_chains, adapt_delta) $optimize() method = "optimize" (e.g., algorithm, iter).

title: Fit a Bayesian meta-analysis model across study designs — meta_did
text: meta_did_fit object. See print.meta_did_fit() summary.meta_did_fit() extracting results. method = "optimize", summary contains MAP point estimates ; sd, lo, hi columns NA.

title: Fit a Bayesian meta-analysis model across study designs — meta_did
text: studies required identification treatment effect. Without , treatment effect confounded time trends (pre-post) baseline group differences (RCT). See vignette("model-details") full discussion model, normalisation, identification.

what: metadid: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs — metadid-package
title: metadid: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs — metadid-package
text: Bayesian meta-analysis framework synthesizing treatment effects across different study designs, including difference--differences (), randomized controlled trials (RCT), pre-post designs. Implements hierarchical models Stan account design-specific biases, missing correlation structures, heterogeneity across studies.

title: metadid: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs — metadid-package
text: Maintainer: Ben Lambert ben.c.lambert@gmail.com

title: Posterior predictive check: CDF comparison — pp_check_cdf
text: fit meta_did_fit object method = "sample". type Character string: "individual" (default) within-study outcome distributions, "summary" distribution study-level treatment effects. study_id Character vector study IDs include. NULL (default) includes studies relevant type. prob Width credible ribbon. Default 0.9.

title: Posterior predictive check: CDF comparison — pp_check_cdf
text: ggplot2 object.

title: Posterior predictive check: CDF comparison — pp_check_cdf
text: Two modes available via type argument: "individual" individual-level study, compares empirical CDF observed outcomes posterior predictive CDF (uncertainty ribbon), faceted study group/time cell. Requires individual-level data fit. "summary" Compares empirical CDF observed study-level treatment effects posterior predictive CDF replicated study effects. single-panel plot provides aggregate calibration check across studies.

what: Set prior distributions for the meta-analysis model — set_priors
title: Set prior distributions for the meta-analysis model — set_priors
text: Construct did_priors object specifying prior distribution population-level parameter. parameter supplied takes default.

title: Set prior distributions for the meta-analysis model — set_priors
code: set_priors(   treatment_effect_mean = normal(0, 10),   treatment_effect_sd = cauchy(5),   time_trend_mean = normal(0, 10),   time_trend_sd = cauchy(5),   rho_mean = normal(0, 1),   rho_sd = normal(0, 0.5),   nu = gamma(2, 0.1),   delta_rct = normal(0, 10),   delta_pp = normal(0, 10) )

title: Set prior distributions for the meta-analysis model — set_priors
text: treatment_effect_mean Prior population treatment effect mean. Default: normal(0, 10). treatment_effect_sd Prior -study SD. Default: cauchy(5). time_trend_mean Prior population time-trend mean. Default: normal(0, 10). time_trend_sd Prior -study time-trend SD. Default: cauchy(5). rho_mean Prior mean Fisher-z transformed pre-post correlation (used hierarchical_rho = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 1). rho_sd Prior SD Fisher-z transformed pre-post correlation (used hierarchical_rho = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 0.5). nu Prior degrees freedom -study heterogeneity (used robust_heterogeneity = TRUE). Default: gamma(2, 0.1). delta_rct Prior RCT design offset relative (used design_effects = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 10). delta_pp Prior Pre-Post design offset relative (used design_effects = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 10).

title: Set prior distributions for the meta-analysis model — set_priors
text: did_priors object.

title: Set prior distributions for the meta-analysis model — set_priors
code: # Use defaults set_priors() #> Prior distributions: #>   treatment_effect_mean ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 10) #>   treatment_effect_sd ~ cauchy(scale = 5) #>   time_trend_mean ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 10) #>   time_trend_sd ~ cauchy(scale = 5) #>   rho_mean ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 1) #>   rho_sd ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 0.5) #>   nu ~ gamma(shape = 2, rate = 0.1) #>   delta_rct ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 10) #>   delta_pp ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 10)  # Override one prior set_priors(treatment_effect_sd = cauchy(2)) #> Prior distributions: #>   treatment_effect_mean ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 10) #>   treatment_effect_sd ~ cauchy(scale = 2) #>   time_trend_mean ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 10) #>   time_trend_sd ~ cauchy(scale = 5) #>   rho_mean ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 1) #>   rho_sd ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 0.5) #>   nu ~ gamma(shape = 2, rate = 0.1) #>   delta_rct ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 10) #>   delta_pp ~ normal(mean = 0, sd = 10)

what: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: Generates individual-level pre/post observations arms set difference--differences studies hierarchical model. gold-standard view data; as_* family functions produce data appear different levels access.

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
code: simulate_meta_did(   n_studies = 20L,   n_control = 100L,   n_treatment = 100L,   true_effect = -0.15,   sigma_effect = 0.03,   true_trend = -0.02,   sigma_trend = 0,   baseline_mean = 0.45,   baseline_sd = 0,   within_sd = 0.12,   rho = 0.5,   seed = NULL )

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: n_studies Number studies. Default 20. n_control Number individuals per study control arm. Default 100L. n_treatment Number individuals per study treatment arm. Default 100L. true_effect Population mean treatment effect. Default -0.15. sigma_effect -study SD treatment effects. Default 0.03. true_trend Population mean time trend. Default -0.02. sigma_trend -study SD time trends. Default 0. baseline_mean Population mean study baselines. Default 0.45. baseline_sd -study SD baselines. Default 0. within_sd Individual-level SD (pre post groups). Default 0.12. rho Pre-post correlation within individuals. Directly parameterises -diagonal bivariate normal covariance matrix. Default 0.5. seed Integer random seed reproducibility. Default NULL.

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: data frame columns study_id, subject_id, group ("control" "treatment"), time ("pre" "post"), value. true study-level parameters attached attribute "true_params".

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: Study-level parameters drawn hierarchically: $$\theta_i \sim \text{Normal}(\texttt{true\_effect},\, \texttt{sigma\_effect}^2)$$ $$\gamma_i \sim \text{Normal}(\texttt{true\_trend},\, \texttt{sigma\_trend}^2)$$ $$b_i      \sim \text{Normal}(\texttt{baseline\_mean},\, \texttt{baseline\_sd}^2)$$ Within study, individual (pre, post) pairs drawn bivariate normal distribution covariance matrix $$\Sigma = \sigma^2 \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ making role \(\rho\) pre-post correlation explicit. mean vector control group \((b_i,\, b_i + \gamma_i)\) treatment group \((b_i,\, b_i + \gamma_i + \theta_i)\).

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
code: dat <- simulate_meta_did(n_studies = 10, true_effect = -0.15, seed = 42) head(dat) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   study_id subject_id group   time  value #>   <chr>         <int> <chr>   <chr> <dbl> #> 1 study_1           1 control pre   0.607 #> 2 study_1           1 control post  0.506 #> 3 study_1           2 control pre   0.724 #> 4 study_1           2 control post  0.578 #> 5 study_1           3 control pre   0.283 #> 6 study_1           3 control post  0.296 attr(dat, "true_params") #> # A tibble: 10 × 4 #>    study_id   theta gamma baseline #>    <chr>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 study_1  -0.109  -0.02     0.45 #>  2 study_2  -0.167  -0.02     0.45 #>  3 study_3  -0.139  -0.02     0.45 #>  4 study_4  -0.131  -0.02     0.45 #>  5 study_5  -0.138  -0.02     0.45 #>  6 study_6  -0.153  -0.02     0.45 #>  7 study_7  -0.105  -0.02     0.45 #>  8 study_8  -0.153  -0.02     0.45 #>  9 study_9  -0.0894 -0.02     0.45 #> 10 study_10 -0.152  -0.02     0.45

title: Validate individual-level study data — validate_individual_data
text: Checks data meets structural requirements use meta_did(). Called automatically inside meta_did(); also useful diagnosing problems fitting.

title: Validate individual-level study data — validate_individual_data
code: validate_individual_data(data)

title: Validate individual-level study data — validate_individual_data
text: data data frame long format one row per observation. Required columns: study_id, design, group, time, value. repeated-measures designs ("" "pp"), subject_id column also required correctly pair pre post observations. Valid designs: "", "rct", "pp".

title: Validate individual-level study data — validate_individual_data
text: data invisibly valid; otherwise stops informative error.

title: Validate summary-level study data — validate_summary_data
text: Checks data meets structural requirements use meta_did(). Called automatically inside meta_did(); also useful diagnosing problems fitting.

title: Validate summary-level study data — validate_summary_data
code: validate_summary_data(data)

title: Validate summary-level study data — validate_summary_data
text: data data frame one row per study. Must contain study_id design columns. Valid designs: "": studies separate pre/post summaries groups. "did_change": studies reporting change means SDs. "rct": RCT studies post-treatment summaries . "pp": Pre-post studies control group. rho column (pre-post correlation) optional "" "pp" designs; missing values trigger hierarchical imputation hierarchical_rho = TRUE.

title: Validate summary-level study data — validate_summary_data
text: data invisibly valid; otherwise stops informative error.
